# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. movies about badass women

**Score:** 65
**Body:** I realized I've never watched a film where women played a decent role. do you have any reccomendations? It doesn't have to be the main character, it can also be a supporting character

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Kill Bill 1 and 2, full stop.
2. Erin Brockovich (2000)
  - “That’s all you have lady. Two left feet and fucking ugly shoes”
3. Aliens

Terminator 2

The Long Kiss Goodnight

You're Next

Furiosa

Atomic Blonde
  - Don't forget Fury Road.  Theron was basically the lead in that movie too.
4. Thelma and Louise

The Silence of the Lambs

Amelie

The Devil Wears Prada

Sicario
5. Jackie Brown.
6. I have a home video where someone told my wife to calm down I can send you
7. The Descent
8. Hidden Figures 

A League of Their Own 

Erin Brockovitch 

Charlie’s Angels 

Black Widow 

Captain Marvel 

The Marvels 

X2 (X-Men)

Wonder Woman

## 2. Movies With The Best Soundtrack?

**Score:** 61
**Body:** Looking for movies with a great soundtrack, something that just makes you bounce your leg the entire movie. Not the actual composition of the film but something with music from bands/artists. My personal pick has to be Transformers: Revenge Of The Fallen. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Singles 

Almost Famous

The Crow

Tron Legacy

Empire Records
2. Forrest Gump, it's basically rock 'n' roll history.
3. Garden State
4. dazed and confused
once upon a time in Hollywood
fast times at ridgemont high
5. Grosse Point Blank.  

The Big Chill
6. Pulp Fiction
7. High Fidelity
8. The Guardians of the Galaxy movies are basically just good mix tapes.
9. Judgment Night (1993)

Lords Of Dogtown (2005)

Last Action Hero (1993)
10. O Brother, Where Art Thou?

Not a soundtrack that will fit everyone’s tastes, but it hits.

## 3. Suggest me a movie guaranteed to reduce my blood pressure and stress?

**Score:** 28
**Body:** Stressful day, i gave myself enlarged arteries in my forehead from all the stress and doctor basically told me to take it as easy as possible so I need a movie that will help. Hit me with your most calming movie 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Peanut butter falcon
  - I read the post wrong thinking he wanted anxiety and stress and this choice baffled me. Haha.
2. Think of them as short films but the TV series "Joe Pera Talks With You" is extremely relaxing.
3. Paddington 2

Chef

Little Women

Fantastic Mr Fox
4. [removed]
5. Blast from the Past. Brendan Frazier, Alicia Silverstone, Dave Foley, Christoper Walken. Sweet, funny, entertaining.
6. Studio ghibli movies always seem to help my mental space. Otherwise try Secret Life of Walter Middy
  - NOT Grave of Firefles. I repeat, NOT GRAVE OF FIREFLIES.
  - My neighbour totoro.

Kiki's delivery service.

Both of these are the most calm films from the studio
7. Whatever you watch, skip the news. Hugs

## 4. Movies where nothing is ever explained?

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Looking for movies where you could absolutely say what the mystery is, but don’t necessarily know the resolution. Asking a big question but not answering it. 

Movies where the nature of the world of the characters’ lives don’t seem to make logical sense and are never explained are also good. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Any david lynch or david kronenberg movie:

Mulholland drive

Lost highway

Naked lunch

Dead ringers 
  - Mulholland Drive. Exactly. 

I needed to google what I'd just watched to make sense of half the movie. That creepy old couple??
2. Donnie Darko
3. FLOW
4. Cube (1997)

Vivarium (2019)
5. Primer, 2004
6. The lobster
7. 2001 A Space Odyssey  (1968)

Nobody understands this movie on first watch.

It is often on lists of most visually beautiful movies of all time.

Bonus: The special effects still look good 57 years later.

Kubrick was a genius.
8. Eraserhead
9. Holy Motors

## 5. War movie suggestions please!

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Been super into war movies for the past couple months. Please share with me your favorites! For and idea I like movies that are pretty historically accurate but it doesn’t necessarily have to be a true story. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Apocalypse Now


Full Metal Jacket 


Life is Beautiful


Au Revoir les Enfants 


Jojo Rabbit


Empire of the Sun


Hope & Glory


Good Morning Vietnam 
2. The Thin Red Line

Paths Of Glory

The Battle of Algiers
3. Black Hawk Down

The Battle of Algiers

Zulu

The Thin Red Line

All Quiet on the Western Front (1930 is the best version)

Platoon

The various Hanks/Spielberg WWII miniseries
4. DAS BOOT!
5. Can’t believe no one has said come and see yet
  - I was going to. But then I thought maybe they don't enjoy getting the hope crushed out of them. Best war movie ever made.
6. WW1> Johnny Got His Gun

WW2> Schindler's List & Saving Private Ryan

Korea> MASH (the film and the series.)

Viet Nam> JFK, Apocalypse Now & Hamburger Hill

Panama> The Panama Deception

Decades of intervention in Latin America> Salvador, The Official Story, MISSING, State Of Siege
7. Hamburger Hill is probably the most accurate and harrowing film about Vietnam ever made. 
8. The Dirty Dozen
9. Flags of Our Fathers

Best Years of Our Lives

Lifeboat

Heroes

Born On The Fourth of July

Yes, most of these are post-war movies.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. An actor you had no expectations for but who turned out to be great.

**Score:** 2314

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Robert Pattinson
  - The Lighthouse, The Batman, and TENET were all great performances I never would have expected from Edward Cullen.
2. I like that he doesn’t play the same character.
  - See, Dwayne? It IS possible!
3. He is the better actor among the WWE lot
  - I agree, but I am also really enjoying Cena in *Peacemaker*.
4. I was hoping this was going to be a sequence where he was wearing glasses that kept getting tinier, but this is good, too.
5. I really liked him in James Bond
  - Was totally kicking Bonds ass, too.
6. He handles comedy really well as Drax and pulling off comedy usually means there's some dramatic talent too.  I think he's going to keep developing and put out some even better performances over time.

## 2. Which single movie showed you an actor's range?

**Score:** 837
**Body:** James mcavoy - split 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Robert Downey Jr in Tropic Thunder :0.
2. Austin Powers - Mike Myers
  - Right? How can a spy be so sexy with such bad teeth.
3. Kathy Bates - Misery. So layered, so awesome. She had like 8 different personalities that I could count.
  - Kathy Bates in Misery vs Waterboy. She has serious range.
4. Colin Farrell in Banshees of Inisherin. His performances shows top quality in humour, dealing with grief and emotional vulnerability. 

For what it's worth, I've always been a fan of his, but I feel this was his best performance and his follow up on The Penguin was a real hot streak for him.
5. Edward Norton - Primal Fear
6. Cristian bale and Hugh Jackman in the prestige
7. Sir Ben Kingsley, Iron Man.
8. Brendan Fraser in Bedazzled

## 3. What movie was like that?

**Score:** 517

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mortal kombat. Got me into metal (fear factory)  and hard electronic etc.

Test!.. your might!.. (epic synths ensue)
2. The phantom menace
  - Duel of the Fates is the best Star Wars song
3. Queen of the Damned. You had to be there 🙃
4. Tron
  - I love tron legacy ost. Still remember it right now
5. Armageddon is a fun movie so maybe doesn’t fit this but the soundtrack is remarkable
6. Godzilla (1998)
7. The Crow: City of Angels. The soundtrack really slaps.
8. Romeo + Juliet.

## 4. Name a scene where it supposed to be emotional but it comes off as goofy and poorly executed

**Score:** 513
**Body:** I don’t know why they do that in this scene, what a waste for Kevin Costner.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Kal-El NO
  - I knew this would be the first comment.
2. It makes sense actually. He saw the script of Batman v Superman and further movies on this verse. And wanted out as soon as possible.
3. Rose Tico slamming into Finn in The Last Jedi and the dumpster fire dialogue/kiss in the wreckage.
4. Yeah, I especially since Superman had the time to just *insanely quickly sprint and meet him halfway or something.
5. Martha
  - „WHY DID YOU SAY THAT NAME???!!“
6. https://youtu.be/S1EsCWrUY84?si=cVRrE5voK-KQtGYy

Worse use of the Whilhelm scream in that scene of Dante's Peak, at the end I just laugh.
7. Nic Cage in a lot of things, like "HOW'D IT GET BURNED?" and "NOT THE BEES!" in the 2006 *Wicker Man* remake
8. Daeth Vader “noooooooooo” when he found out queen amadala died

## 5. Which family do you know of that keeps pumping out acting talents?

**Score:** 323

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Pretty sure they are just shat out by Stellan
  - The one true *Nepo Daddy*
    - Tbf, they turn out as pretty good actors. There's a fine line between amazing mentor and nepo baby
      - I was just quoting Stellan from a recent Vulture article where he called himself a “nepo daddy” lol
2. Why do all of the Skarsgards look completely unrelated to each other
  - He has a different mother compared to the others.
3. Wake up babe, a new Skarsgard just dropped.
4. At this point we should all be asking ourselves if Stellan is our dad.
5. They even have their own [Theme Song](https://www.instagram.com/reel/DJFdauny7lh/?igsh=MnRwMDVsb2lzYmoz)
6. Alexander is absolutely amazing in Murderbot

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. An actor who consistently delivers exceptional performances yet remains underrated: Jake Gyllenhaal

**Score:** 23
**Body:** Jake Gyllenhaal is, in my opinion, one of the most compelling actors of his generation. Across a wide range of roles — from the psychologically intense Lou Bloom in Nightcrawler to the physically demanding boxer in Southpaw, the enigmatic duality in Enemy, or the emotionally intricate Ennis Del Mar in Brokeback Mountain — he exhibits extraordinary versatility and depth.

What sets him apart is his complete immersion into characters, both physically and psychologically. Despite this, mainstream recognition often eludes him, and he rarely receives the same attention as contemporaries of comparable talent.

For those reasons, I consider him one of the most consistently exceptional yet underappreciated actors working today.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This whole thread feels like a fever dream. Jake Gyllenhaal is properly rated and is not underrated. Ive heard and seen people list him in their top five actors all time. To the other commentator, a lot of people say Tilda Swinton is one of the most versatile actors of all time. Comparing her to Gal Gadot of all people has me baffled.
  - Yes, thank you. Gyllenhaal remains a highly bankable star, (and as an old person, he was right from the jump, Donny Darko, Brokeback Mountain and even October Sky he was lauded with praise) and I just don’t think anyone who’s played a marvel villain can be “underrated” since the industry itself has ranked them high enough to anchor a franchise. 


A properly underrated actor would be Justin Theroux. He wrote tropic Thunder, crushed the leftovers, worked with Lynch twice, but I don’t think anyone really thinks about him or talks about him.
  - This is just a typical reddit moment at this point. People will watch the godfather for the first time and post "omg, you have to see this hidden gem!" Like they're the only one to discover it.
    - On Reddit there are like 20 movies that get talked about constantly and everything else is either total shit or underrated
  - The Reddit definition of underrated is "we stopped talking about it for fifteen minutes."  Jake Gyllenhaal doesn't get a lot of press today and it has nothing to do with his acting, it's because his filmography over the last 5-10 years has been very weak.  Nocturnal Animals was probably the last really great movie he was in and that was 2016.  Since then he was in a movie called The Covenant which is apparently very good and an okay Apple TV series.  What else has he been in?  Spider-Man?

It's the same reason people don't talk about Naomi Watts, a much more extreme example. Everyone in the world knows she's an amazing actress but unfortunately her career has been in the gutter for ten years and she's stuck in a cycle of taking on shit films to pay the bills.  What recognition does OP expect?  People constantly talking about a guy who isn't in any good movies anymore?
  - The other aspect is that he's known to be a super intense, kind of weird dude. I think he could have more 'accolades' matching the impressive output of his filmography if he really wanted to play that game.
    - I found out recently that he is legally blind. Got 20/1250 eyesight or something.
2. Gyllenhaal (and Jackman) climbed quite high in my esteem when I saw Prisoners.

When months later I saw Nightcrawler, that was it. It's up there with DDL in There Will be Blood.

We often lack the imagination to accept that another actor could have handled the role. Of course actors are not copies of each other, they take part in the creative process. Different actors, different films, in a way.

But seriously, Nightcrawler with someone else than Gyllenhaal ? Yeah maybe a young Nicholson or someone of that stature could have sold it - differently, but it could have been equally compelling. Not many actors could have bring the level of tension this high and not be ridiculous.
3. The problem is he's always poorly cast as the leading man, when he's more creepy like Joaquin. He is absolutely perfectly cast in Nightcrawler and Nocturnal Animals, and should do more of those.
4. I would not say he is underrated. I think when you see him name in credits you know he is going to give a good performance. 

Allot of it can be luck and film choices you make, Creating momentum. 

I think the prince of Persia stalled his career.

## 2. Amour — Understated, Yet Brilliant Sequence

**Score:** 20
**Body:** There’s a short sequence in Amour that seems inconsequential, but nevertheless captures the absurd manner in which we conduct our life by ignoring the ever present reality of death. 

Just after Anne has her second stroke, her daughter Eva visits her. As Anne is laying upon the bed, appearing as if she is on the verge of death, Eva discusses her financial situation and the various options/rates she is considering before purchasing a property. 

There’s something about this scene/juxtaposition that really struck a nerve. It’s as if Haneke was communicating the way in which we waist our previous lives — this impermanence existence. Despite being in the premise of her dying mother, Anne can helper but be concerned about her finances. Maybe I’m being too harsh on Eva, but there’s ultimately something absurd, and perfectly natural, about her actions throughout this sequence and her lack of making contact with the present moment. It’s the exact action that comes from denying the reality of death. 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Haven't seen the film in a long time and don't remember it well, but I get what you're saying. In defense of Eva: older, sick people can seem close to dying for a verrrry long time - too long for younger people's attention spans... 

What I've seen around me is that many people are mainly preoccupied with their own lives when their empathy is put to the test. And maybe some part of that indifference stems of an unwillingness to face their own future (end) and all kinds of related existential questions, like who will take care of them, etc. I think in the past, when families would live (and die) together, frailty and death were part of life more than now.

## 3. How do you find repertory screenings?

**Score:** 4
**Body:** We’ve got [this handy little site](https://screenboston.com) in Boston that tracks the next month or so of showtimes, but it isn’t easily searchable. That’s fine here, where only a half-dozen theaters regularly show repertory cinema, but what about bigger cities? If you’re in New York, you’ve got [Screen Slate](https://www.screenslate.com), which is completely overwhelming and a real burden to navigate. I imagine there’s something similar, and similarly opaque, in Los Angeles. Is there anything that covers multiple cities? Anything searchable?

If I were dying to see one particular old film in the theater and were happy to travel for it, would I have any options other than googling *”touch of zen” showtimes* or whatever and laboriously sorting through all the old listings and other false hits? If it were a film that’s never been back in theaters since release (say, *Apollo 11,* as far as I know), would I have any recourse better than searching for a needle in a haystack?

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I live in Los Angeles. There are things like Revival Hub which gathers information screenings around LA and puts them on an easy to access website.

What I do is that I follow certain cinemas or screening spaces and subscribe to their newsletters and follow them on instagram. I also pay attention to screening organisations that theatre hop. When I attend a screening, I meet other people there who might be interested in the same things I am and they may know of other spaces and screenings I do not know about. So I may learn of a new space in Los Angeles and will take note (these days I think this happens more and more frequently that places open and close and reopen).

The thing about repertory screenings is that it's up to the programmers to decide what's being shown. Maybe if you really want to see A Touch of Sin and you think you know of a good space and the film follows their mission statement, suggest it to the programmer and they can look into the screening costs etc. Programmers are almost always happy to chat, discuss and hear other people's ideas.
2. There honestly aren't *that* many revival theaters in LA. I just cycle through the sites of the ones I like at the beginning of each month: New Beverly, Vista, Academy Museum, American Cinemateque, Vidiots, Secret Movie Club (although they've been inactive for a while).

## 4. Boogie Nights (1997): overrated film

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I've found a lot of people online who refer to PTA's 1997 film as a life-changing experience, a cult classic, a comedic masterpiece. At first I thought the hype was conditioned by its release year, but the more I remember past films that cover similar motifs  the more I realize PTA does really struggle to make remarkable or transgressive stories.

When it comes to films like Licorice Pizza, The Master or Boogie Nights, for this matter, he dives into characters with a complex life in unusual settings, however, the final product fails to amuse. Yeah, great actors and great performances, but no breaking point, no coup de grâce.

Is Boogie Nights about a convoluted sex work industry ? Paul Verhoeven already did it great with Showgirls (1995). Is it about substance abuse and narcissism? The Doors (1991) was a raw depiction of it. Is it about voyeurism and sexual freedom? Brian de Palma and Cronemberg covered those nicely. It's a film portraying drugged porn producers yet it feels so vanilla and safe. 

But I get the most important part of the argument is the search for a family, a place to belong and dream chasing. But the characters struggle individually and there's no emotional payoff to the whole thing. Its comedy part fails as well. My only favorite part was the third act's gun shootings. 

What are your thoughts on this film?




### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Its cool if you didn't like it, but the movies you used for comparison of the tropes is completely off. The Doors is a biopic of the band and nothing more. Showgirls is a satire, as are most of PV's films. Boogie Nights is PTA doing what PTA does best, weaving lives together. Life is messy, life is chaotic, life can be complicated, life does not always have happy endings or really any meaning other than what you give it.
2. If you want to say that something done before shouldn’t be done again, cinema would have fallen off around the 50s.

It doesn’t matter if it’s been told before. It’s never been told by that person before.
  - I don’t really understand OPs take.
    - OP doesn’t either
  - Reminds me of the ‘Great Stories’ argument: basically, almost every film, book, etc is a recombination of a handful of archetypal narratives. Saying Boogie Nights isn’t special because its themes have appeared before is true in a literal sense, but it misses the point: what makes a film stand out isn’t inventing a brand-new story, it’s how it tells it; the characters, tone, technique, performances. BN is a masterclass in directing on these fronts, while bringing a very distinct style, imo
  - AMEN
3. >Is Boogie Nights about a convoluted sex work industry ? Paul Verhoeven already did it great with Showgirls (1995).

If this whole post was rage-bait, good job I guess. But if the premise of your argument relies on the idea that Showgirls (a notoriously terrible movie that ended several careers) is in actuality some secret masterpiece...yeah I don't know. I think you're struggle to find common ground with most people here.
  - Showgirls is a film that has been pretty widely reclaimed as a great film over the past 15 years. Its now mostly looked at through the lenses of camp and satire, as well feminist and queer film theory. There have been 30th anniversary screenings happening all over this year. Its an *incredibly* popular and bankable rep programmer for indie/arthouse cinemas.

While I agree that comparing the two films is a bit silly, saying that Showgirls is universally regarded as a terrible film is definitely not a good argument.
    - Where do you see this wide reclamation happening? I'm aware it has very, *very* vocal fans who have been trying to make it happen for years, and yes it's enjoyed as *camp*, but I would argue that's through an ironic lens of "so bad it's good". Not actually a re-evaluation as a great piece of cinema.

I mean hell, we literally have the term ***"showgirls-bad"*** for a terrible movie that's enjoyable to watch -- often because it so egregiously and unwittingly misses the mark. It's a midnight screening movie akin to Rocky Horror or to a lesser degree I Know Who Killed Me.

>saying that Showgirls is universally regarded as a terrible film is definitely not a good argument

Good thing I didn't say that then.
  - I mean, Showgirls is not terrible, its great. Elizabeth Berkley basically died for our sins, which is too bad, but she's lived long enough to get her critical reappraisal along with the movie. That said, it's not Boogie Nights.

---
